ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for cheese mixed with za'atar herb blend; mapped to broader cheese food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for cheese-za'atar combination; A00LC (processed cheese preparations) is the closest defensible parent analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT maps to the cheese substance class; no specific za'atar-cheese combination term exists.

main_ingredients: white cheese (akkawi or similar brined cheese)
za'atar herb blend (thyme, oregano, sumac, sesame seeds)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cheese and za'atar is approximately 35g, equivalent to a small portion spread on flatbread or served as a mezze accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese & Za'Atar — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cheese & Za'Atar (G00010369). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for white brined cheese (akkawi-type) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat fractions for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — cheese and dairy products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese-za'atar combination. GI estimated at 27 based on the GI of full-fat white cheese / soft cheese (GI ~27, University of Sydney GI Database). Za'atar herb blend and olive oil contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not meaningfully alter the GI. Glycemic load per 35g serving = (27 × 8.85 × 0.35) / 100 = 0.84, rounded to 0.8. Classified as low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279mg per 100g — more than double the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid this food; even a 35g serving delivers approximately 448mg sodium, representing nearly 20% of the 2300mg daily limit, and regular consumption will substantially elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (8.85g/100g) and low GI (~27) are favourable, but high saturated fat (approx. 13.5g/100g) from dairy may impair insulin sensitivity with habitual consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cheese and za'atar is acceptable in small portions (30–40g) for people with type 2 diabetes due to its low glycaemic impact, but saturated fat intake should be monitored and total daily dairy fat kept within recommended limits to avoid worsening insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~13.5g/100g) and very high sodium (1279mg/100g) are dual cardiovascular risk drivers
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should consume this food only occasionally and in very small portions (20g or less), as the combination of high saturated fat and extreme sodium significantly raises LDL cholesterol and blood pressure, both established CVD risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dairy saturated fat (~13.5g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); extreme sodium (1279mg/100g) worsens joint oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this food; the high dairy saturated fat load and extreme sodium content outweigh the anti-inflammatory polyphenol contribution of za'atar herbs and olive oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese and Za'atar is a Middle Eastern mezze preparation consisting of white brined cheese — typically akkawi, nabulsi, or a similar soft-to-semi-firm Levantine cheese — blended or served alongside za'atar, a traditional herb-and-spice mixture composed of dried thyme, oregano, sumac, toasted sesame seeds, and salt, often bound with olive oil. The dish is common across Lebanon, Palestine, Jordan, Syria, and Israel, and is widely served in Middle Eastern restaurants and households in Singapore and the broader diaspora. The texture is creamy and slightly crumbly from the cheese, with a herbaceous, tangy, and nutty flavour profile imparted by the za'atar blend. It is typically eaten as a spread on flatbread (khubz or pita), as part of a mezze platter, or as a breakfast accompaniment. Nutritionally, it is calorie-dense at 307 kcal per 100g, with high protein (16.09g), high fat (23.06g, predominantly saturated from dairy), moderate carbohydrate (8.85g), and very high sodium (1279mg). Calcium content is exceptionally high at 1375mg/100g, making it a significant dietary calcium source. The food is low GI (~27) due to its minimal carbohydrate and high fat-protein matrix. Clinically, the extreme sodium content makes it unsuitable for hypertension management, and the high saturated fat load raises cardiovascular and inflammatory concerns. Za'atar herbs contribute polyphenols (rosmarinic acid, thymol, carvacrol) with antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory properties, though these do not offset the sodium and saturated fat burden at typical serving sizes.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in cheese za'atar?
Is cheese za'atar good for blood pressure?
Middle Eastern cheese spread with herbs

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010369) with confidence 0.95. The HPB record is an approved primary source for Singapore-context foods. Saturated fat estimated at ~13.5g/100g based on typical dairy cheese composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for akkawi/white brined cheese, consistent with the total fat value of 23.06g/100g. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for full-fat soft cheese.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (13.5g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for white brined cheese (akkawi-type), as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is consistent with the total fat of 23.06g/100g and typical dairy fat profiles. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and micronutrient data for this specific cheese-za'atar preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a combined dish entry as of 2026. 3. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB database entry (G00010369). The za'atar herb blend contributes negligible fibre at typical inclusion levels in this preparation. 4. Food group corrected from input 'Meat and alternatives / Beef' to 'Dairy and eggs / Cheese-based dishes' — the input classification is clearly erroneous for a cheese-based Middle Eastern dish. 5. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on full-fat soft/white cheese from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for cheese-za'atar combination exists in approved sources. 6. This dish is listed in the Singapore HPB database, indicating it is served in Singapore's Middle Eastern food establishments; multilingual names in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not applicable as this is a Middle Eastern (not Singaporean) dish — the G6 multilingual rule applies to Singaporean dishes only, and this dish is classified as Middle Eastern cuisine. 7. Ontology foodex2_match_type corrected from 'analogous' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the scoring model.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial record built from HPB source data; corrected food group from 'Meat and alternatives/Beef' to 'Dairy and eggs/Cheese-based dishes'; added multilingual aliases and health context.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from non-standard values ('broader') to 'closest_parent' for foodon and snomed_ct.

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous' to 'closest_parent' to conform strictly to the allowed enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as flagged by validator feedback. All three ontology match_type fields now use only valid enumeration values.
